questions.q: Which three components define a web origin under the Same-Origin Policy?
When does a browser send a CORS preflight request?
questions.a: Before a cross-origin request that uses a non-simple method or header

questions.q: Which XSS type stores the payload on the server?
What does the HttpOnly cookie attribute prevent?
What SameSite value blocks a cookie on all cross-site requests?
What CSP feature lets you allow a specific inline script?
Why does CSRF still work even when SOP exists?
questions.a: The browser will send the victim's cookies with a cross-site request unless the app defends against it.

questions.q: Why is HttpOnly not a full defense against XSS?
questions.a: It blocks cookie theft, but injected script can still act on behalf of the user.

questions.q: What is the difference between reflected and stored XSS?
questions.a: Reflected payloads come from the current request; stored payloads are saved and served later.

questions.q: Why does CORS not replace SOP?
questions.a: CORS is an opt-in exception mechanism, while SOP is the default browser isolation rule.

questions.q: What does the X-Frame-Options or frame-ancestors CSP directive protect against?
questions.a: Clickjacking by preventing the page from being embedded in an unwanted frame.
It lets an attacker make the server fetch internal or privileged network resources.

questions.q: Why is SameSite=Lax helpful but not enough as a sole CSRF defense?
questions.a: It blocks many cross-site subrequests, but sensitive flows can still need explicit CSRF tokens.

questions.q: Why is a CSP nonce stronger than a broad allowlist for inline scripts?
questions.a: The nonce ties execution to server-generated randomness instead of trusting all inline code.

questions.q: Why do modern applications combine multiple defenses for XSS?
questions.a: Input validation, output encoding, CSP, and HttpOnly each cover different failure modes.

questions.q: Why can open redirects and SSRF become chained attacks?
questions.a: An attacker can use redirect or fetch behavior to reach internal endpoints indirectly.

questions.q: Why is origin-based security easier for browsers than for servers alone?
questions.a: Browsers enforce the policy automatically, while servers must implement validation themselves.

questions.q: What tradeoff does a strict CSP impose on front-end code?
questions.a: It reduces injection risk but can make inline scripts and third-party integrations harder to manage.
